Every founder building a "too early" startup today should study Pets.com — not to learn "don't spend on marketing," but to learn that you can't will infrastructure into existence with venture capital alone.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much money did Pets.com lose in total?

Pets.com burned through approximately $300 million in total investor capital between its founding in 1998 and its liquidation in November 2000. This included roughly $82.5 million raised in its IPO, $50 million from Amazon, and additional venture capital from firms like Hummer Winblad. The company lost $147 million in just the first nine months of 2000 alone. When Pets.com liquidated, its stock price had fallen from $11 at IPO to $0.19 — a decline of over 98%. Nearly all invested capital was unrecoverable.

What happened to the Pets.com sock puppet after the company shut down?

After Pets.com announced its closure in November 2000, the rights to the sock puppet character were purchased by Bar None, a subprime auto lending company based in Virginia. Bar None featured the puppet in its own commercials, rebranding it as a spokesperson that had "survived the dot-com bust" and was now helping people with bad credit get car loans. It was a remarkably self-aware piece of marketing. The puppet had a brief second career before fading from public view, though it remains one of the most recognizable brand mascots of the early internet era.

Was Pets.com the biggest dot-com failure?

In terms of raw dollars lost, no — companies like Webvan (grocery delivery, burned through $830 million) and eToys ($190 million IPO, bankrupt within two years) arguably destroyed more capital. However, Pets.com is the most iconic dot-com failure because of the sock puppet's cultural visibility, the Super Bowl ad, the speed of its collapse (268 days from IPO to liquidation), and the dramatic irony that online pet retail eventually became a hugely successful industry. Pets.com became shorthand for the entire dot-com era's excess, whether or not it was technically the largest failure.

Could Pets.com have survived if it had done things differently?

Probably not, given the constraints of 2000. The fundamental problem — shipping heavy, low-margin pet products profitably using turn-of-the-millennium logistics infrastructure — wasn't solvable with better management or more conservative spending. The company could have survived longer with less marketing burn, but survival isn't the same as viability. The market conditions that make online pet retail profitable (advanced logistics, digital marketing, high internet penetration, subscription models) simply didn't exist yet. Even Amazon, with all its resources, didn't crack profitable pet supply delivery until well into the 2010s.

posts.title: The Tamagotchi Made Millions of People Cry Over a Plastic Egg (And Changed Tech Forever)
posts.author: 404 Memory Found
posts.excerpt: Bandai sold 40 million Tamagotchis in two years. Schools banned them. People buried them in real cemeteries. Here's the full story of the toy that taught us to love pixels.

seo.metaDescription: Bandai sold 40 million Tamagotchis in two years. Schools banned them. People held funerals for them. The full wild story of the digital pet revolution.

posts.body:
The year was 1996, and a small egg-shaped plastic toy was about to make millions of people around the world genuinely, emotionally attached to a collection of pixels on a liquid crystal display. Not attached in the way you're attached to your phone. Attached in the way you're attached to a living thing. Kids cried when their Tamagotchi died. Adults scheduled their lunch breaks around feeding times. Schools started confiscating them like contraband. A pet cemetery in Cornwall, England started burying them in actual graves. And Bandai, the Japanese toy company behind it all, sold over 40 million units in the first two years alone. The Tamagotchi wasn't just a toy. It was the first time technology made us care about something that wasn't real, and it completely rewired how an entire generation thought about digital relationships.

A Turtle, a Teenage Girl Market, and a Tiny Egg

The idea for Tamagotchi came from a man named Akihiro Yokoi, who worked at a company called WiZ. He saw a TV commercial about a boy trying to bring his pet turtle on a trip and thought: what if you could carry a pet in your pocket? Not a real one. A digital one that needed actual care and attention. He brought the concept to Bandai in 1995, and they rejected it. So Yokoi did something smart. He went to Shibuya, one of Tokyo's busiest shopping districts, and studied what teenage girls were buying. He noticed that almost every handheld electronic device on the market was designed for boys. There was a massive gap. If he could make something "kawaii" (cute) enough, he could own an entirely underserved market.

He partnered with Aki Maita, a Bandai employee who became the public face of the project. Together, they designed a device shaped like an egg (the name "Tamagotchi" combines the Japanese words "tamago," meaning egg, and "uotchi," meaning watch). The concept was simple but brilliant: you hatch a digital creature, and it needs you. You feed it, clean up after it, play with it, discipline it, and put it to sleep. Neglect it, and it gets sick. Ignore it long enough, and it dies. For real. No undo button. No respawn.

The first Tamagotchi went on sale in Japan on November 23, 1996. It retailed for about 1,980 yen (roughly $17). Within weeks, it was sold out everywhere.

The Frenzy That Nobody Predicted

When Bandai launched the Tamagotchi in the United States on May 1, 1997, FAO Schwarz in San Francisco sold its entire stock of 3,000 units by 3:00 PM on the first day. Not by end of business. By mid-afternoon. Parents were lining up before stores opened. Scalpers were flipping them for $100 or more. By the end of 1997, Bandai had sold over 10 million units worldwide, and the number kept climbing. By 1998, that figure hit 40 million, including 12 million in North America alone.

The pricing was part of the genius. At $17.99, it was cheap enough that parents would buy one on impulse but expensive enough to feel like a real purchase for a kid. Compare that to a Game Boy, which cost $69.99 in 1997 (plus $30 per game). The cost of 90s tech adjusted for inflation makes the Tamagotchi look like the bargain of the decade.

The cultural footprint was massive. Tamagotchi keychains were everywhere. Kids wore them around their necks. Teenagers clipped them to backpacks. Japanese businesswomen were reportedly canceling meetings to care for their digital pets. In November 1997, the Swedish Retail Institute named it the Christmas Gift of the Year. Yokoi and Maita won the 1997 Ig Nobel Prize for Economics, a tongue-in-cheek award that basically called them the mother and father of the digital pet revolution.

Why Schools Went to War with a Plastic Egg

The problem with Tamagotchi was built into its design. Your pet could die in less than half a day if you didn't check on it. That meant kids weren't putting them away during class. They were sneaking glances under their desks, pressing buttons during tests, and having full-on emotional breakdowns when their creature died mid-lesson. Teachers went from annoyed to furious in a matter of weeks.

Schools across the United States, United Kingdom, and Japan started banning them outright. California and New York were among the first, with teachers citing them as the single biggest classroom distraction they'd ever dealt with. Some schools treated Tamagotchis like they were cell phones (which, in 1997, most kids didn't even have yet). Confiscation bins filled up. Parents wrote angry letters demanding their kids' toys back. It was chaos.

But here's the thing that made Tamagotchi bans different from, say, banning Game Boys or trading cards. Kids weren't just playing a game. They felt responsible for a digital life. Taking away someone's Tamagotchi during school hours meant their pet might die while it sat in a teacher's desk drawer. That created a level of emotional distress that no school administrator had planned for. Some kids started leaving their Tamagotchis with friends who had a free period, creating informal daycare networks. In Japan, actual paid Tamagotchi daycare services popped up for adults who couldn't check their devices during work.

Funerals for Pixels

When a Tamagotchi died, it didn't just display "Game Over." The screen showed a small angel or a gravestone, depending on the version. For a lot of kids (and more adults than you'd expect), that was devastating. The emotional response was so widespread that it spawned an entire mourning culture around digital pet death.

In January 1997, two teenage girls traveled to a pet cemetery in Pontsmill, Cornwall, England to bury their Tamagotchis, named Sid and Arty. Fourteen-year-old Danielle Perren told reporters she wanted to remember her pet "as he was," not as he'd be if she just reset the device. The cemetery owner, Terry Squires, soon started receiving Tamagotchis from around the world. Switzerland, Germany, France, Canada, the United States. He fenced off a dedicated section of his cemetery for digital pet burials.

Online, memorial websites popped up where you could post eulogies, record your Tamagotchi's age at death, and list the cause of death. Some of these sites still exist today, abandoned but intact, frozen in late-90s HTML with tiled backgrounds and animated GIF candles. If you want a snapshot of how seriously people took this, spend five minutes browsing the Tama Talk memorial forums. It's equal parts funny, sweet, and a little bit haunting.

The Tamagotchi effect (that's what researchers actually call it) became a legitimate area of academic study. Psychologists wanted to understand why humans could form genuine emotional bonds with a creature that was, objectively, just a few lines of code running on a chip the size of a fingernail. The consensus? Our brains don't fully distinguish between real and simulated care relationships when the feedback loop is convincing enough. You feed it, it grows. You neglect it, it suffers. That's enough for your brain to start treating it as real.

The Crash, the Comeback, and the 100 Million Milestone

Like most 90s fads, the original Tamagotchi craze burned white-hot and then faded fast. By 1999, sales had dropped off dramatically. Bandai's stock price took a hit. The toy aisles moved on to the next thing. For a few years, Tamagotchi looked like it would become just another relic of 90s nostalgia, filed next to AOL Instant Messenger and slap bracelets.

But Bandai never actually stopped making them. The Tamagotchi Connection launched in 2004 with infrared communication, letting two devices interact with each other. Your Tamagotchi could visit a friend's Tamagotchi, play together, and even have offspring. The Connection line sold well, especially in Japan, and kept the brand alive through the mid-2000s.

Then came the Tamagotchi Uni in 2023, which added Wi-Fi connectivity and a color screen. Suddenly you could connect your Tamagotchi to the internet, visit a virtual world called the Tamaverse, and interact with players globally. In 2024, Bandai released the Tamagotchi Connection 20th Anniversary edition, bringing back the classic infrared gameplay with modern touches and over 50 characters to raise.

In August 2025, Bandai announced that cumulative worldwide shipments had officially surpassed 100 million units. About 49% of those were sold in Japan, 33% in the United States, 16% in Europe, and 2% across the rest of Asia. That same year, Tamagotchi was inducted into the World Video Game Hall of Fame. Not bad for a toy that most people assumed died in 1999.

What Your Old Tamagotchi Is Worth Now

If you still have an original 1996 or 1997 Tamagotchi sitting in a drawer somewhere, it might be worth checking. A used, loose original typically sells for $40 to $50 on eBay. But if you have it in the original packaging, especially unopened, that number jumps to several hundred dollars. A bundle of three original English-language Tamagotchis recently sold for about $2,250.

The real money is in rare variants. A Tamagotchi Plus sold for $5,000 in 2021. Limited edition models like the Pizzara-kun have been listed at $1,600, and certain Tamagotchi Plus Tama Depa versions go for over $3,000. The collector market is real, driven largely by millennial nostalgia and the same logic that turned first-edition Pokemon cards into five-figure investments.

But most people don't want to sell their Tamagotchi. They want to play it again. And that's exactly why Bandai's modern revival strategy is working so well. The new models retail for around $30 to $60, which is roughly double the original price but still impulse-buy territory. Bandai understood something important: nostalgia isn't just about remembering. It's about reliving. And you can't relive the Tamagotchi experience by watching a YouTube video about it. You have to hold the egg in your hand and hear that familiar beep.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who actually invented the Tamagotchi?

Two people deserve the credit. Akihiro Yokoi of WiZ came up with the original concept after seeing a commercial about a boy and his pet turtle. He partnered with Aki Maita at Bandai to develop and produce it. Maita was the public face of the project for months, but both were recognized together when they won the 1997 Ig Nobel Prize for Economics.

How many Tamagotchis have been sold worldwide?

Over 100 million. Bandai hit the milestone in August 2025, nearly 29 years after the original launch. The first 40 million sold in just the first two years, which gives you a sense of how intense the initial craze was.

Why were Tamagotchis banned in schools?

Because a Tamagotchi could die in less than 12 hours without care, kids couldn't put them down during class. They'd check them under desks, press buttons during tests, and have emotional meltdowns when their digital pet died. Schools in the US, UK, and Japan started confiscating them, treating them the same way schools today treat phones.

Can you still buy a new Tamagotchi in 2026?

Yes. Bandai has been releasing new models consistently. The Tamagotchi Uni (2023) added Wi-Fi and a color screen. The Tamagotchi Connection 20th Anniversary edition came out in 2024 with infrared multiplayer features. A new model called Paradise is expected in mid-2025. Prices range from about $30 to $60 depending on the model.

Were people actually burying Tamagotchis in real cemeteries?

Yes. A pet cemetery in Cornwall, England started accepting Tamagotchi burials in January 1997 and received devices from multiple countries. Online memorial sites also appeared where owners could post eulogies and record their pet's cause of death. Some of those sites are still online today, frozen in 90s web design.

What's the most expensive Tamagotchi ever sold?

Rare variants regularly sell for thousands of dollars. A Tamagotchi Plus sold for $5,000 in 2021, and certain limited editions like the Tama Depa version have been listed above $3,000. Even a basic original in unopened packaging can fetch several hundred dollars. The collector market mirrors what happened with vintage Pokemon cards and retro game cartridges.

posts.title: When MTV Actually Played Music: How the Biggest Channel in Cable Abandoned Its Own Name
posts.author: 404 Memory Found
posts.excerpt: MTV went from launching careers with music videos to airing Jersey Shore reruns. The real story of how Music Television stopped being about music.

seo.metaDescription: MTV went from playing 'Video Killed the Radio Star' to zero music videos. The real timeline of how Music Television abandoned its own name.

posts.body:
The meeting lasted exactly one sentence. On August 1, 1981, a voice announced "Ladies and gentlemen, rock and roll," and MTV started broadcasting music videos into American living rooms for the first time. The first clip they played was "Video Killed the Radio Star" by the Buggles. Fourteen years later, the network was the most powerful force in popular music, pulling in advertising rates that made competitors sick, launching careers overnight, and reaching over 70 million households. And then, gradually and then all at once, MTV decided it didn't want to play music anymore. The story of how a channel called Music Television became a channel that plays almost zero music is one of the strangest identity crises in media history. It's also a story about money, about attention spans, and about what happens when the thing you built gets destroyed by the thing you helped create.

Building a Cultural Weapon (1981-1991)

MTV didn't have an easy start. When it launched in 1981, the channel was only available in a handful of cable markets, mostly in New Jersey and the surrounding areas. Major record labels were skeptical. Why would they give away their music for free on television? The early playlist was dominated by whoever would actually hand over their videos: mostly British new wave acts and rock bands. The Buggles, Pat Benatar, Rod Stewart, and a lot of acts most people have forgotten about.

The turning point came in 1983 when Michael Jackson's "Thriller" video premiered on MTV. It wasn't just a music video. It was a 14-minute short film directed by John Landis, and it turned MTV from a niche cable curiosity into a cultural event. By 1984, advertising revenue was hitting $1 million per week, and MTV's audience had grown to 22 million viewers between the ages of 12 and 24. It was the highest-rated basic cable network in America.

That same year, the first MTV Video Music Awards aired from Radio City Music Hall. Madonna performed "Like a Virgin" while emerging from a 17-foot wedding cake in a see-through dress. She accidentally lost her shoe, ended up crawling across the stage, and flashed her underwear on live television. Rolling Stone would later rank it the sixth most outrageous moment in VMA history. The VMAs immediately became appointment television and one of the most important awards shows in music.

1992: The Year Everything Changed (But Nobody Noticed)

On May 21, 1992, MTV premiered a show called The Real World. The concept was almost absurdly simple: put seven strangers in a house, film everything, and see what happens. No script. No competitions. No prize money. Just people being people, with cameras rolling.

The show was not an immediate blockbuster, but it pulled ratings roughly three times higher than the music videos it replaced in its time slot. Three times. For MTV's executives, that was the number that changed everything. Music videos were relatively expensive to produce (the label made them, but MTV had to fill 24 hours of airtime with programming around them). Reality TV was cheap. You needed a house, some cameras, and seven people willing to have their lives broadcast to millions. The cost-per-viewer math was brutal and obvious.

The Real World didn't just change MTV. It created an entirely new genre of television. Survivor, Big Brother, The Bachelor, every dating show, every house show, every competition show with confessional interviews owes something to what MTV started in that New York loft in 1992. But for MTV specifically, it was the first crack in the music-first foundation. The executives had found something that got better ratings for less money. The question was never whether they'd make more of it. The question was how fast.

The Golden Age Nobody Wants to Admit Was Ending (1993-1997)

The mid-90s were when MTV was at its most culturally dominant, and also when the seeds of its transformation were already planted. On the surface, music still ruled. This was the era of MTV Unplugged, one of the most important music series in television history.

MTV Unplugged launched in 1989 and ran regularly through 1999, but its peak was squarely in the mid-90s. The concept was simple: take popular artists, strip away the electric instruments and production, and have them perform acoustically in an intimate setting. The results were often revelatory. Eric Clapton's Unplugged session in 1992 produced an album that sold 26 million copies worldwide. But the defining moment came on November 18, 1993, when Nirvana taped their session at Sony Studios in New York.

Kurt Cobain, already battling health issues and the pressures of fame, chose to fill the set list with deep cuts and obscure covers instead of playing Nirvana's biggest hits. They covered the Meat Puppets, Lead Belly, and David Bowie. The only real hits were "Come as You Are," "Polly," and "All Apologies." The album, released after Cobain's death in November 1994, debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 with 310,500 first-week sales and eventually moved over 8 million copies in the US alone. It won Best Alternative Music Performance at the Grammys. It remains one of the most important live albums ever recorded.

But while Unplugged was making music history, the rest of MTV's schedule was quietly shifting. Road Rules launched in 1995. More reality programming filled the gaps. From 1995 to 2000, MTV slashed its music video programming by nearly 40 percent. The profits, though? Those grew by about 25 percent per year through the mid-to-late 90s, with cash flow margins around 40 to 41 percent. The math was working. The identity crisis was just getting started.

TRL: The Last Time MTV Was a Music Channel

On September 14, 1998, a 25-year-old former radio DJ named Carson Daly stepped in front of cameras at MTV's Times Square studios and launched Total Request Live. The show's format was deceptively simple: count down the top 10 most-requested music videos of the day, as voted on by viewers. Play clips of each video. Talk to fans. Occasionally bring in artists for interviews and performances.

TRL became a phenomenon almost immediately. By 1999, it was pulling nearly 800,000 viewers per day, making it MTV's flagship program. The Times Square studio had floor-to-ceiling windows facing the street, and on any given afternoon, hundreds of screaming fans would crowd Broadway outside, holding signs and hoping to get on camera. When the Backstreet Boys or *NSYNC or Britney Spears showed up, the crowd sometimes grew into the thousands. NYPD had to set up barricades. Traffic stopped. It was controlled chaos, every single weekday.

TRL turned Carson Daly into a household name and accelerated the careers of artists who dominated the late-90s pop explosion. But it also did something else: it proved that MTV's audience cared less about watching full music videos and more about the spectacle around them. The show played clips, not full videos. The real draw was the countdown drama, the fan interaction, the in-studio interviews. Music was the backdrop, not the main event. In retrospect, TRL was both MTV's last great music moment and the proof that its audience was ready to move on from music entirely.

How the Napster Revolution Pulled the Rug Out

While MTV was wrestling with its own identity, the music industry was getting hit by a truck. Napster launched in June 1999, and within a year, millions of people were downloading music for free. By 2001, CD sales were falling off a cliff. Record labels were in panic mode, slashing budgets for everything, including the music videos that had been MTV's core content.

This mattered enormously for MTV. In the channel's early days, record labels essentially provided free programming. They made the videos, they paid for the production, and MTV played them. It was a symbiotic relationship: MTV gave artists exposure, and labels gave MTV content. But as labels cut video budgets, the quality and quantity of new music videos dropped. The pipeline of free, high-quality content that MTV had relied on for almost 20 years started drying up.

At the same time, a new platform was emerging that would make the entire concept of music television obsolete. YouTube launched in February 2005, and within two years, anyone could watch any music video they wanted, whenever they wanted, for free. The core value proposition of MTV (we'll show you music videos you can't see anywhere else) evaporated overnight. Why would you sit through an hour of programming, waiting for your favorite video to come on, when you could just type the song name into a search bar?

The Reality Takeover in Hard Numbers

Once MTV realized that reality programming got better ratings at lower cost, the pivot accelerated. The numbers tell the story better than any analysis:

In 2000, MTV was still airing about 8 hours of music videos per day. By 2008, that number had dropped to 3 hours. By 2010, it was less than an hour, mostly in the early morning when almost nobody was watching. The rest of the schedule was wall-to-wall reality TV.

And the reality shows were pulling massive numbers. Jersey Shore premiered on December 3, 2009, averaging 1.375 million viewers for its first episode. By the second season premiere in July 2010, that number had exploded to 5.25 million viewers. Jersey Shore's season finales were regularly pulling ratings that no music video block had matched in years.

The economics were undeniable. A music video block required licensing deals, VJ salaries, and careful programming. A reality show needed a camera crew, a few microphones, and people willing to be filmed doing questionable things in a beach house. The profit margins on reality TV were enormous compared to music programming. MTV's parent company, Viacom, wasn't in the business of preserving cultural legacy. They were in the business of making money. And reality TV made a lot of it.

What MTV Actually Lost

The thing that gets lost in the "why did MTV stop playing music" conversation is what MTV actually did that nobody else could replicate. It wasn't just about showing videos. MTV was a cultural filter. It decided what was cool. It broke new artists. It created shared moments that an entire generation experienced simultaneously.

When Madonna shocked the VMAs, everyone saw it. When Nirvana played Unplugged, everyone talked about it the next day. When TRL counted down the top 10, you could call your friends and argue about whether the Backstreet Boys or *NSYNC deserved the number one spot. These were shared cultural experiences that required everyone to be watching the same channel at the same time. Streaming and YouTube killed that. Not because the content was worse, but because the experience became individual instead of collective.

MTV also served as a quality filter. Not every music video got on MTV. Getting your video played was an achievement that signaled you'd crossed a threshold of relevance. YouTube has no such filter. Anyone can upload anything. That's democratic and wonderful in many ways, but it also means there's no central authority declaring "this matters, pay attention." The music video as a cultural event died when MTV stopped being the gatekeeper.

MTV's Music Channels Finally Go Dark

In a move that surprised absolutely nobody who'd been paying attention, Paramount Global (MTV's parent company) shut down several of MTV's remaining music-focused channels in recent years. MTV Classic, MTV Hits, and other music-branded channels were either repurposed or quietly pulled off the air. The reaction online was a mix of mourning and resignation. "End of an era" was the most common phrase, but in truth, the era had ended years earlier. These channels had been running on fumes and reruns, watched by almost nobody.

MTV's flagship channel still exists, but its programming in 2026 consists almost entirely of reality shows, game shows, and reruns. The channel that launched with "Video Killed the Radio Star" became the thing it once replaced. Radio didn't kill music video stars. MTV did, by deciding they weren't profitable enough to keep around.

Why This Still Matters

MTV's story isn't just a media history curiosity. It's a template for how platforms die. First, you build something people love. Then you discover that something else makes more money. Then you gradually replace the thing people love with the thing that makes more money. Then your audience leaves, and you're left with neither the love nor the money.

Netflix is arguably going through a version of this right now, shifting from prestige content to cheaper reality programming. Social media platforms cycle through the same pattern, prioritizing engagement metrics over the content that made people sign up in the first place. MTV was the original case study, and nobody learned the lesson. The channel that taught the world how to watch music taught the media industry how to abandon its core audience. Both lessons stuck.

The irony that hangs over all of it is the first video they ever played. "Video Killed the Radio Star." They thought it was about someone else.

Frequently Asked Questions

What was the first music video played on MTV?

"Video Killed the Radio Star" by the Buggles, which aired on August 1, 1981, at 12:01 AM. The choice was deliberate and kind of perfect. The second video played was "You Better Run" by Pat Benatar.

When did MTV stop playing music videos?

It was a gradual process, not a single moment. In 2000, MTV was still playing about 8 hours of music videos per day. By 2008, that was down to 3 hours. By the early 2010s, music videos had been almost completely replaced by reality shows and other programming. TRL, the last major music-focused show, was canceled in September 2008.

What was TRL and why was it so popular?

Total Request Live was a daily countdown show where viewers voted for their favorite music videos. Hosted by Carson Daly starting in September 1998, it peaked at nearly 800,000 daily viewers in 1999. The show's Times Square studio became a destination, with hundreds (sometimes thousands) of fans crowding the street outside every afternoon. It was MTV's last truly music-centered hit.

How much money did MTV make at its peak?

MTV's profits grew approximately 25 percent per year through the mid-to-late 1990s. The network maintained cash flow margins of 40 to 41 percent in 1995-1997, which was considered exceptional for the industry. Advertisers paid premium rates to reach MTV's young audience, and cable operators bundled it into standard packages that guaranteed recurring revenue.

Did The Real World really invent reality TV?

It didn't invent the concept (there were earlier examples like PBS's "An American Family" in 1973), but The Real World, which premiered on May 21, 1992, is widely credited with launching the modern reality TV genre. Its format of putting strangers in a house with cameras became the template for Survivor, Big Brother, The Bachelor, and countless others.

Why did MTV switch to reality TV?

Money. Reality shows pulled ratings roughly three times higher than music video blocks, and they cost significantly less to produce. When record labels started cutting music video budgets in the early 2000s (partly due to the Napster-driven revenue collapse), the pipeline of free content that MTV relied on dried up. YouTube's launch in 2005 eliminated the need for a dedicated music video channel entirely. The economics pushed MTV toward reality TV, and the numbers backed it up.

What was MTV Unplugged and why did it matter?

MTV Unplugged ran regularly from 1989 to 1999 and featured popular artists performing acoustic versions of their songs. Eric Clapton's 1992 session produced an album that sold 26 million copies. Nirvana's 1993 session, recorded five months before Kurt Cobain's death, became one of the most iconic live performances in rock history. The resulting album sold over 8 million copies in the US and won a Grammy.

Is MTV still on the air in 2026?

Technically yes, but it bears almost no resemblance to its original form. The flagship channel still broadcasts, mostly airing reality shows, game shows, and reruns. Several of MTV's music-focused sub-channels (MTV Classic, MTV Hits) have been shut down or repurposed by parent company Paramount Global. The channel that launched by playing "Video Killed the Radio Star" hasn't been about music in over a decade.

posts.title: What Happened to BlackBerry? The Smartphone King That Lost Its Crown
posts.excerpt: From the undisputed king of enterprise smartphones to a footnote in tech history, BlackBerry's meteoric rise and devastating fall mirrors the rapid evolution of mobile technology itself.
posts.body:
The Beginning: Research In Motion and the Wireless Revolution

In 1984, Mike Lazaridis and Douglas Fregin co-founded Research In Motion in Waterloo, Ontario, with a simple yet audacious vision: to build wireless data devices that would change how business professionals worked. Jim Balsillie joined the company in 1992, investing $125,000 for a one-third stake and becoming co-CEO alongside Lazaridis. At the time, mobile phones were luxury items reserved for the wealthy, weighing as much as bricks and offering little functionality beyond voice calls. Lazaridis, a University of Waterloo engineering student with a passion for wireless technology, recognized a vast untapped market of businesspeople who needed constant access to their messages and data.

Throughout the late 1980s and early 1990s, RIM focused on pager and wireless modem technology, products that seemed destined for obscurity as the world moved toward cellular networks. But Lazaridis had foreseen this transition. He built the company's foundation on deep expertise in wireless protocols, data compression, and encryption—skills that would prove invaluable when the smartphone era arrived.

The BlackBerry 850/857: Birth of a Smartphone Category

In 1999, RIM released the BlackBerry 850, the first true BlackBerry device that combined a mobile pager with wireless email access. These weren't the first devices to attempt email on a handheld, but they were the first to make it actually work reliably. They featured a small physical keyboard, monochrome displays, and most importantly, bidirectional wireless email through RIM's own network infrastructure.

The appeal to enterprise customers was immediate and overwhelming. For the first time, executives could stay connected to their email even while away from the office. No more frantic searches for payphones. No more missed messages. The devices carried a premium price, but corporations gladly paid for the productivity gains.

"The BlackBerry wasn't just a device. It was a security blanket for the connected executive class. It meant you were important enough to be reached anywhere, anytime."

By the early 2000s, BlackBerry had become synonymous with mobile email. The distinctive shaped devices became status symbols in boardrooms and law offices across North America. RIM's revenue climbed steadily, but the company remained relatively unknown to the general consumer market.

The Golden Era: Pearl, Curve, and Mainstream Domination

In 2006, RIM released the BlackBerry Pearl, a pivotal moment in the company's history. The Pearl was the first BlackBerry to target not just enterprise users but regular consumers. It featured a smaller form factor, a camera, and crucially, a more affordable price point. The Pearl introduced the SureType input method—a hybrid between the full QWERTY keyboard found on other BlackBerrys and a traditional phone keypad.

But it was the BlackBerry Curve, launched in 2007, that truly brought BlackBerry to the mainstream. The Curve combined the Pearl's consumer appeal with a full QWERTY keyboard in a more compact package. With pricing around $200 on carrier contracts, the Curve became accessible to millions. By 2008, the Curve and its successor models were flying off store shelves.

RIM's revenue exploded during this period. In fiscal year 2009, RIM generated $11.1 billion in revenue, up from just $594 million five years earlier. The company's market capitalization exceeded $80 billion, placing it among the most valuable technology companies in the world. By 2009, BlackBerry controlled over 50% of the U.S. smartphone market according to some estimates, making it the dominant mobile platform in America.

The Celebrity Effect and the Obama Presidency

By the late 2000s, BlackBerry had achieved something remarkable: it became the phone of choice for celebrities, politicians, and cultural influencers. When President Barack Obama took office in January 2009, he famously fought to keep his BlackBerry despite Secret Service security concerns. This endorsement from the highest office in the United States reinforced BlackBerry's image as the sophisticated, serious choice—the phone for people who got things done.

The physical keyboard became almost a point of pride for BlackBerry users. While the iPhone, launched in 2007, had pioneered the touchscreen interface that seemed more futuristic, BlackBerry users argued that their keyboards were faster for typing, more tactile, and less prone to accidental inputs. The phenomenon of "BlackBerry thumb" became a cultural reference—the idea that intensive BlackBerry use could cause thumb strain from constant typing on the device's small keyboard.

RIM's co-CEO Jim Balsillie was a master of investor relations and played the celebrity BlackBerry culture perfectly. The company seemed unstoppable, a Canadian success story that had conquered the world.

The iPhone Moment and the Touchscreen Miscalculation

Everything changed on June 29, 2007, when Apple released the first iPhone. Steve Jobs's device represented a fundamental reimagining of the smartphone—a large touchscreen with no physical buttons, a revolutionary interface, and later an App Store ecosystem that would change computing forever.

BlackBerry executives initially dismissed the iPhone. Mike Lazaridis reportedly questioned how the iPhone could possibly succeed without a keyboard. The physical keyboard advocates doubled down on their position. This proved to be one of the most consequential strategic miscalculations in technology history.

As the iPhone matured, the iPad launched in 2010, and Google's Android operating system gained market traction, the smartphone landscape fundamentally changed. By 2011–2012, touchscreen devices had become standard. The physical keyboard, once BlackBerry's trump card, now seemed antiquated to younger users who had grown up with touchscreen interfaces.

"We believed our product was so good we didn't need to fundamentally change it. The market proved us catastrophically wrong."

The Storm: A Desperate Gamble

In November 2008, RIM attempted to fight back against the iPhone with the BlackBerry Storm, the company's first touchscreen-only device. The Storm was meant to be RIM's answer to Apple's challenge.

Instead, it was a disaster. The device featured a "SurePress" screen that required users to physically click the entire display to register inputs, a design decision that made the typing experience awkward and error-prone. The touchscreen felt sluggish compared to the iPhone's responsive capacitive display. Battery life was poor. The software felt incomplete.

Customers returned the Storm in droves. Reviews were scathing. For the first time, BlackBerry fans felt betrayed by their beloved company. Rather than establishing BlackBerry as a serious touchscreen competitor, the Storm's failure reinforced Apple's dominance and made BlackBerry seem desperate and out of touch.

The PlayBook Tablet and the Widening Gap

In April 2011, RIM released the PlayBook tablet, a 7-inch device running BlackBerry Tablet OS (based on QNX). Like the Storm, the PlayBook was meant to prove RIM's relevance in a changing market. Also like the Storm, it failed spectacularly.

The PlayBook launched without native email, calendar, or contact support—basic functionality that seemed incomprehensible for a productivity device. To access email, users had to tether the PlayBook to a BlackBerry smartphone via Bluetooth—a bizarre requirement. The app ecosystem was virtually nonexistent compared to Apple's App Store or the growing Google Play Store.

The PlayBook's failure was particularly damaging because it revealed a fundamental strategic confusion at RIM. The company couldn't decide whether it was a consumer company, an enterprise company, or both. RIM eventually took a $485 million write-down on unsold PlayBook inventory.

BlackBerry 10: Too Little, Too Late

In January 2013, after multiple delays, RIM finally released BlackBerry 10, a completely new operating system built on the QNX microkernel. The OS was technically impressive—it featured a gesture-based interface called BlackBerry Flow, strong multitasking capabilities, and a modern architecture.

BlackBerry 10 devices, including the all-touch Z10 and the keyboard-equipped Q10, were reasonably well-reviewed by tech critics. For a brief moment, some observers believed RIM might have a path to redemption.

But BlackBerry 10 arrived at precisely the wrong moment. By 2013, iOS and Android had dominated the market for years. The app ecosystem gap was insurmountable. Most major applications prioritized iOS and Android first, treating other platforms as afterthoughts. BlackBerry users who had left years earlier saw no compelling reason to return.

More importantly, RIM's enterprise customers—the foundation of BlackBerry's original success—had already migrated to iPhones and Android devices. BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) policies that had seemed unthinkable in the BlackBerry era had become industry standard.

John Chen and the Pivot to Software

In November 2013, John Chen became CEO of the struggling company, which had officially renamed itself BlackBerry Limited earlier that year. Chen inherited a company in freefall. BlackBerry's smartphone market share had collapsed to under 3% globally.

Chen's strategy was straightforward: get out of the hardware business and focus on what BlackBerry did well—security software and services for enterprise customers. BlackBerry's encryption and security reputation, built over decades, remained valuable even as its phones became irrelevant. The company acquired Cylance, an AI-based cybersecurity firm, for $1.4 billion in 2019. BlackBerry also developed QNX software for the automotive industry, and its platform now powers infotainment systems in over 235 million vehicles worldwide.

This pivot made financial sense, but it represented the complete abandonment of BlackBerry's core identity as a phone maker.

The Final Devices and the End of an Era

BlackBerry released several more phones after the BlackBerry 10 era, manufacturing through a partnership with TCL. These included the KEYone (2017) and the KEY2 (2018), both running Android with BlackBerry's signature physical keyboard. These were aimed at loyal holdouts who couldn't imagine life without physical keys.

Even these devices found only a niche audience. TCL's licensing agreement ended in August 2020, and no new BlackBerry phones have been manufactured since. OnwardMobility, a Texas-based startup, announced plans to revive the BlackBerry phone brand in 2020 but shut down in 2022 without ever shipping a device.

BlackBerry formally ended support for its legacy BlackBerry OS, BlackBerry 7.1, and BlackBerry PlayBook OS on January 4, 2022. Devices running those operating systems could no longer make calls, send texts, or use data—a quiet death for devices that had once been indispensable to millions.

"In 2010, BlackBerry seemed unstoppable. By 2022, the lights went out entirely. The company went from owning the future to being irrelevant in a single decade—one of the most dramatic reversals in technology history."

The Legacy of BlackBerry

What happened to BlackBerry was not simply a failure to innovate. It was a failure to recognize how fundamentally the smartphone market had shifted. BlackBerry's strength—its secure, encrypted, keyboard-focused approach designed for businesspeople checking email—became its weakness in a world where touchscreen devices had won, where app ecosystems mattered more than native features, and where consumer preferences overrode enterprise tradition.

The company had invented the smartphone category as we know it and owned it longer than almost any other company. Its design language, security focus, and understanding of mobile workers' needs had been validated thousands of times over. But it couldn't adapt quickly enough when the goalposts moved.

RIM's executives were not incompetent. Mike Lazaridis and Jim Balsillie built a genuinely innovative company. The problem was that they trusted in the correctness of their vision too much. They believed that BlackBerry's physical keyboard, security features, and email-first approach were so intrinsically valuable that the market would never abandon them.

The iPhone and Android proved them catastrophically wrong. Not because BlackBerry phones were poorly designed—they weren't. But because the market revealed that people would trade premium security and physical keyboards for multitouch interfaces, app ecosystems, and devices that did everything, not just email and messaging.

Today, BlackBerry Limited still exists as a software and cybersecurity company. Its QNX operating system is embedded in hundreds of millions of vehicles. Its cybersecurity products protect enterprise endpoints around the world. But the phones—the devices that once made Presidents and CEOs inseparable from their BlackBerrys—are gone forever.

FAQ: BlackBerry Questions Answered

Can I still use a BlackBerry phone today?

Legacy BlackBerry OS devices are essentially non-functional since January 4, 2022, when BlackBerry ended support. They cannot make calls, send texts, or use data on most networks. Android-based BlackBerry phones (KEYone, KEY2) still work as Android devices but no longer receive software updates. Many 3G networks that older BlackBerry devices relied on have also been shut down.

Why did the physical keyboard matter so much?

For business email users, a physical keyboard allowed for faster, more accurate typing without looking at the screen. BlackBerry users reported significantly higher typing speeds compared to early touchscreen devices. However, most users eventually adapted to touchscreen keyboards, and the smartphone market prioritized screen size and app access over keyboard speed.

Did BlackBerry really control 50% of the US smartphone market?

At its peak around 2009, BlackBerry held approximately 50% or more of the U.S. smartphone market according to various industry reports. The exact figure varies by source and methodology. However, this was when overall smartphone penetration was much lower—many Americans still used feature phones.

Could BlackBerry have survived if it had embraced Android earlier?

Possibly, but it would have meant abandoning BlackBerry's core differentiation. By the time BlackBerry shifted to Android with the Priv in 2015, Samsung, LG, and others had already captured the Android market. The keyboard niche proved too small to sustain a major phone manufacturer.

What do Mike Lazaridis and Jim Balsillie do now?

Lazaridis became a prominent philanthropist and investor, founding Quantum Valley Investments in Waterloo to fund quantum computing research. He also co-founded the Perimeter Institute for Theoretical Physics. Balsillie became a public intellectual focused on data governance, intellectual property policy, and Canadian innovation, founding the Centre for International Governance Innovation (CIGI).

Is BlackBerry truly gone, or does the company still exist?

BlackBerry Limited still exists as a software company focused on cybersecurity (Cylance), enterprise endpoint management, and automotive software (QNX). It no longer manufactures smartphones. OnwardMobility's attempt to revive BlackBerry phones failed in 2022. As a phone maker, BlackBerry is finished.

posts.title: What Happened to Kodak? They Invented Digital Photography—Then Died
posts.excerpt: Kodak literally invented the digital camera in 1975—then spent 30 years burying it. The company that dominated photography for a century became the ultimate cautionary tale in corporate self-destruction.
posts.body:
The Founder's Vision: "You Press the Button, We Do the Rest"

George Eastman founded the Eastman Kodak Company in 1888 with a deceptively simple mission: democratize photography. At the time, photography was an expensive, technically complex hobby dominated by professionals using large-format cameras and glass plates. Eastman's innovation wasn't the camera itself—it was the business model. He introduced roll film and the Kodak camera, a simple box that anyone could use. His marketing slogan became legendary: "You press the button, we do the rest." For $25, consumers could buy a camera pre-loaded with film for 100 exposures. When finished, they mailed the entire camera back to Kodak, who developed the prints, reloaded the film, and shipped it back. Photography became accessible to ordinary people for the first time.

This wasn't mere marketing genius—it was the birth of the razor-and-blades business model. Kodak made money not primarily from camera sales, but from film and developing services. For over a century, this model worked flawlessly. By 1976, Kodak commanded 90% of the U.S. film market and 85% of the camera market. The company employed over 145,000 people globally at its peak in the late 1980s. Rochester, New York, became a company town, with the iconic Kodak Tower standing as a monument to the firm's dominance.

The Peak of the Film Empire: A Century of Dominance

From 1888 through the 1990s, Kodak was essentially synonymous with photography. The company didn't just make film—it defined the entire aesthetic and culture of how families preserved memories. "Kodak moment" entered the English language as a phrase for a perfect, photogenic scene worth capturing. Kodak Tri-X film became the professional standard for black-and-white photography. Kodachrome, introduced in 1935, revolutionized color photography and remained iconic for decades—Paul Simon even immortalized it in a 1973 song.

The business was enormously profitable. Kodak's revenue peaked at approximately $16 billion in 1996. The company's profit margins were extraordinary because film was a consumable product with high repeat purchase rates. A family might buy a camera once, but they'd buy film rolls repeatedly for years. The margins on film development were equally attractive. Kodak's annual reports from the 1980s and 1990s read like a company utterly confident in its perpetual dominance.

During these decades, Kodak invested heavily in research and development, spending billions on innovation. The company had first-rate scientists and engineers. It seemed impossible that Kodak could ever be displaced from its throne.

The Invention That Would Doom Them: The First Digital Camera

In December 1975, a young engineer named Steve Sasson was working in a Kodak laboratory in Rochester. Sasson was experimenting with CCDs (Charge-Coupled Devices)—light-sensitive semiconductors that could convert light into electronic signals. He had a radical idea: what if he used a CCD to create an entirely electronic camera that captured images digitally rather than on film?

Sasson built the first digital camera—a crude prototype that weighed about 8 pounds (3.6 kg), recorded black-and-white images at 0.01 megapixels (10,000 pixels, in a 100×100 grid) to a cassette tape, and took 23 seconds to capture a single image. By any objective measure, it was technically inferior to film cameras of the era. But Sasson had created something conceptually revolutionary: he had proven that you could capture photographic images with electronics instead of chemicals.

When Sasson presented his prototype to Kodak management, the response was telling. Executives asked when this technology might become competitive with film. Sasson estimated 15 to 20 years—which proved remarkably accurate. But the follow-up question revealed everything: "Why would anyone ever want to look at their pictures on a television set?" The executives couldn't envision a world without prints.

So Kodak shelved the technology. Sasson continued to work on digital imaging at Kodak throughout the late 1970s and 1980s, and he would eventually hold patents on many foundational digital photography technologies. But the company never aggressively commercialized his invention. When Canon, Nikon, Sony, and others entered the digital camera market in the 1990s, Kodak had squandered a generational head start.

The Rational Delusion: Why Kodak Couldn't Pivot

Kodak's refusal to embrace digital photography wasn't stupidity—it was rational corporate self-interest in catastrophic disguise. The company's entire financial structure depended on film and developing services. Digital cameras didn't require film, and digital images didn't require printing. If Kodak aggressively pushed digital photography, they would cannibalize their own high-margin film business.

This is the classic "innovator's dilemma," a concept later popularized by Harvard professor Clayton Christensen in his 1997 book. The sensible financial move, from a quarterly earnings perspective, is to protect your existing revenue stream. Kodak's executives were running a wildly profitable company. Asking them to voluntarily destroy that profitability in service of an uncertain future seemed irrational at every board meeting.

Throughout the 1990s, Kodak did release some digital cameras, including the DC series starting with the DC40 in 1995. But these efforts were halfhearted and underfunded compared to the company's film marketing. Meanwhile, Japanese manufacturers were moving aggressively into the market.

The company also made a costly strategic bet on the wrong technology. In 1996, Kodak co-launched the Advanced Photo System (APS), marketed as Advantix—a new film format that was supposed to be the future of consumer photography. Kodak invested hundreds of millions in APS development. By the time Advantix cameras hit store shelves, digital cameras were becoming viable consumer products. Kodak had poured resources into a technology that would be obsolete within years.

The Collapse: From Dominance to Bankruptcy

The transition happened with stunning speed. Global digital camera shipments surged from about 4.7 million units in 2000 to over 100 million by 2008. Film sales plummeted in parallel. By 2005, Kodak was already bleeding—the company's stock, which had peaked around $94 per share in 1997, had fallen below $30.

Kodak's stock continued its freefall. By 2011, shares traded below $1. On January 19, 2012, Kodak filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, listing $5.1 billion in assets against $6.75 billion in liabilities. The company that had once been one of the most recognized brands in the world was insolvent.

The bankruptcy devastated Rochester. Kodak's workforce, which had peaked at over 145,000 employees globally (with roughly 60,000 in the Rochester area alone in the 1980s), had been shrinking for years through waves of layoffs. By the time of the bankruptcy filing, fewer than 17,000 employees remained. Entire neighborhoods that had been built around Kodak workers emptied out.

"Kodak's problem wasn't that it failed to see digital photography coming. It saw it coming before anyone else—because it invented it. The problem was that Kodak's entire economic engine depended on digital photography not happening."

The Antonio Perez Era: Attempted Reinvention

Antonio Perez became Kodak's CEO in 2005, having previously served as president of HP's consumer business. Perez aggressively pivoted the company away from consumer photography toward commercial printing, specifically commercial inkjet systems and packaging. He also pushed into functional printing and enterprise document management.

Perez's strategy made theoretical sense: exit the dying consumer photography business and become a commercial printing technology company. Kodak divested subsidiaries, sold off patent portfolios, and invested in professional printing equipment. The company sold its digital imaging patents to a consortium of companies including Apple, Google, Facebook, Amazon, and Microsoft for $527 million in 2013.

But the pivot was too late and the debt too large. In 2007, Kodak still generated $10.3 billion in revenue. By 2011, that had fallen to $6.1 billion. Legacy costs, pension obligations, and the speed of the film business collapse overwhelmed the company before the printing pivot could gain traction.

Kodak emerged from bankruptcy in September 2013 as a much smaller company focused on commercial printing, packaging, and professional services. The photography giant that had shaped how the world captured memories was, for all practical purposes, gone.

The Strange Pharmaceutical Pivot of 2020

In July 2020, the Trump administration announced that Kodak would receive a $765 million loan under the Defense Production Act to manufacture pharmaceutical ingredients for generic drugs, including components related to hydroxychloroquine. Kodak's stock surged from about $2 to over $60 in two days on the news.

The deal quickly unraveled. Reports emerged that Kodak executives had received stock options shortly before the public announcement, and the stock had risen suspiciously on July 27—the day before the official news. The SEC opened an investigation, and the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation put the loan on hold. While the SEC ultimately did not charge Kodak executives, two individuals connected to the deal were later charged with insider trading in 2023. The loan was never finalized, and Kodak's stock crashed back down. The episode became a symbol of pandemic-era corporate opportunism.

Kodak Today: The Relic and the Revival

Kodak still exists today, though it bears almost no resemblance to the company that once dominated global photography. Under CEO Jim Continenza, the company focuses on commercial printing solutions, advanced materials, and brand licensing. Kodak's annual revenue for 2024 was approximately $1.04 billion—a 93% decline from its 1996 peak.

Remarkably, Kodak film is still manufactured and sold. Kodak Professional Portra, Kodak Gold, Kodak Tri-X, and Kodak Ektar—films that have been in production for decades—are still available at specialty retailers and online. There has even been a modest resurgence of interest in analog photography, particularly among younger photographers drawn to the aesthetic qualities of film. Kodak has benefited from this trend, and some discontinued film stocks have been relaunched in recent years. The company brought back Kodak Ektachrome E100 slide film in 2018 after discontinuing it in 2012.

The irony is profound. Kodak's refusal to embrace digital in the 1980s and 1990s destroyed the company. Yet the only business that survived from Kodak's golden era is the film business itself—now serving a small but passionate niche market of analog enthusiasts. The digital cameras that killed film have themselves been largely displaced by smartphones, adding yet another layer of irony to the story.

The Legacy: A Lesson in Disruption

Kodak's story has become perhaps the most frequently cited case study in business schools worldwide on the subject of disruptive innovation. The company that invented the technology that would destroy it failed to capitalize on that technology. This is often simplified as a story of corporate stupidity and arrogance. The reality is more complex and more instructive.

Kodak didn't fail because its executives were stupid. Many understood the threat posed by digital photography. Internal reports from the 1980s explicitly warned about the digital transition. The problem was structural: Kodak's financial system, quarterly earnings targets, Wall Street expectations, and massive pension obligations all incentivized protecting the existing film business rather than cannibalizing it. A CEO who aggressively pushed digital photography in 1985 would have destroyed short-term earnings and likely would have been fired by the board before long-term benefits materialized.

This is the tragedy of the Kodak story. The company's failure wasn't a matter of vision or intelligence. It was a matter of institutional structures that made rational decisions at each step—protecting quarterly earnings, maximizing return on capital, avoiding excessive risk—but collectively produced a catastrophic result.

Today, as waves of technological disruption continue to reshape industries—artificial intelligence, electric vehicles, renewable energy—executives look at Kodak as the ultimate warning. The question every leader in a dominant company asks is: "How do we avoid becoming the next Kodak?" The answer, as Kodak's ghost reminds us, is much harder than it appears.

FAQ

Did Steve Sasson actually invent the first digital camera?

Yes. In December 1975, Sasson completed the first fully electronic digital camera at Kodak's labs in Rochester using a Fairchild CCD sensor, an analog-to-digital converter, and a digital cassette recorder. The prototype captured 0.01-megapixel black-and-white images. While others contributed to related technologies, Sasson is credited as the inventor of the digital camera. He was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2011 and received the National Medal of Technology and Innovation from President Obama in 2009.

Why didn't Kodak just switch to making digital cameras?

Kodak's business model depended on the recurring revenue from film sales and photo developing. Digital cameras eliminated both revenue streams. Additionally, Kodak had no competitive advantage in digital camera hardware—Japanese electronics companies like Sony, Canon, and Nikon had superior expertise in electronics manufacturing. Kodak did sell digital cameras in the late 1990s and 2000s, but struggled to compete on price, features, and quality.

How much was Kodak worth at its peak?

Kodak's revenue peaked at approximately $16 billion in 1996. The company's stock price peaked around $94 per share in early 1997, giving it a market capitalization of roughly $31 billion. Adjusted for inflation, that's equivalent to approximately $60 billion in 2026 dollars.

Is Kodak film still being made?

Yes. Kodak continues to manufacture several film stocks including Portra 160 and 400, Gold 200, Tri-X 400, Ektar 100, and Ektachrome E100. Film has experienced a niche renaissance among photographers and artists who appreciate its unique aesthetic qualities. Kodak has invested in keeping its film manufacturing facility in Rochester operational to meet this demand.

What happened to Steve Sasson?

Sasson worked at Kodak for over 30 years, retiring in 2009. He was awarded the National Medal of Technology and Innovation by President Barack Obama in 2009 for his invention of the digital camera. He was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2011 and the Consumer Electronics Hall of Fame in 2001. He holds numerous patents related to digital imaging technology.

Could Kodak have survived if they had embraced digital earlier?

Possibly, but it would have required a fundamentally different company. Fujifilm, Kodak's chief film rival, managed to survive the digital transition by aggressively diversifying into healthcare, cosmetics, and advanced materials—leveraging its chemical expertise in non-photography applications. If Kodak had pursued a similarly aggressive diversification strategy in the 1990s while gradually managing the film business decline, the company might have survived in a different form. The path that would not have worked was simply becoming a digital camera company—that market was eventually destroyed by smartphones too.
